speaker: interviewer
text: November 5th, 1995. We are conducting an interview with a survivor Fred Hoffman, here at his home in Denver, Colorado. Fred, let me take you back a little bit to before you came to America, before you arrived in New York. You mentioned that you wanted to go to Israel. You wanted to go to Palestine. What happened? How come you never ended up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened is, that we were studying those chayalim from Israel. Those, those teachers were teaching us Hebrew songs. They were telling us a lot of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life in, in Palestine. And I joined a little youth organization. It called like a kibbutz. We were very young children there, and probably ages of 20, 18, 22, 25. And we were studying Hebrew. They were teaching us Hebrew. They were teaching us Isra-- Israeli songs. And they were preparing us to go to Palestine. I was in a kibbutz there. And we, we must have been about a group of about 50, 60 young children, youngsters-- not children, but youngsters. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, my brother got married in, in Italy. And he told us to hold off until he will see what will happen with him when he comes to America. My kibbutz was ready to go to emigrate to Magdala, to Israel, to Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They put them on the ship Exodus. But I said to the leader that I am not going to go. I think I'm going to pull out. He got really angry at me. And he says, well, if that's the way  you feel, that's too bad. But I thought that you will all go together, he said. I told him, though, that my brother told me to hold off and not to, not to rush yet, not-- to wait a little longer. I said, I'm not pulling out. I say, I'll join you later on maybe. But I just have to see what's going to happen. The group, that whole group we were together, they were put on the ship Exodus. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were trying to get into Palestine. They didn't make it. They got caught. And they all send them to Cyprus, on an island there. From Cyprus, they came back, all of them. And they said that I did pretty smart that I pulled out, because they went through a lot of hell in that time, being on the, on the, on the ocean and then being in Cyprus and coming back They just couldn't make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your decision was a good one.

speaker: interviewee
text: My decision was just then I made up my mind that I will come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived at Ellis Island. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother took me. I was working there. And then, after two years being in New York, as I said, I was happy in one way and not too happy. Because there was some fear in me. It's up till today. When I am in a crowd somewhere, I, I hate to be standing in a line. I hate to be in a big crowd. It's in me. It's, it's, it's a fear from, from that camp, which it will never go away from me. I came out to visit a couple friends here. And I kind of liked it. So I went back to New York. And I quit my job. And I came out to Denver. I moved here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a 1950-- '53. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got a job at a department stores, working in the tailor shops. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was a single guy. We were a-- met some more friends over here. We were going out on the weekends. We had good company. I was pretty happy. And I was here five years, just working. Then I buy a newspaper one Sunday morning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I see in the paper a picture of a Greek-Jewish family emigrated to Denver, Colorado. The daughter will continue with her education and the son. They brought their grandma with them, the father and the mother, the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- and they said, oh, the father is a tailor of, of that family. And they will try to find him a job and try to settle him up-- settle him here in Colorado. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It went by a few days. And I thought, I better-- I would like to go and meet that family, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was also ready to settle down and start a family of my own. I went by there. And I met them. I walked in there. And I met them. They didn't speak English. And I didn't speak Greek. So it was-- a friend of theirs was there in the house. He was speaking English and Greek. So he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: An interpreter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Interpreting-- yeah. And I was kind of like the girl. I was very happy to meet them and talk to them and try to make them a little more like at home, Make them feeling good. I said to the guy what was the interpreter to tell her father, the girl's father, that I will try to put him to work there where I worked, in the department store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, fine. He explained to him. I talked to my boss. And he says, sure, bring him down. I said, but he doesn't speak English. He says, well, we will communicate somehow. Because they needed some more tailors in that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meantime, I start dating the girl, Miriam. We were going out. And we really fell in love. Thank God, she's a sweet girl. And I start dating her more and more often. I used to go there. I used to eat with them. They liked me. And her father start going with me to work-- took him there, come home. And they accepted me like in the family, like I am theirs. Then I proposed to her. And they said, yes, OK. I ask her father for permission. This is the European way of if you want to marry the daughter, you ask the father. And then if the father, yes, OK. You know, that's the way it goes. That's kind of a custom [? that I was-- ?] and we are engaged. And three months later, we were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 18th, 1956.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: From that day on, I built my own family. My happiness came to me. We were very happily married. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They liked me very much. I liked them. And we got along very well. And then, thank God, my children were born. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First was Hanna, now Sylvia. Then I have a son, Tony, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which you just met over here. My son is in San Francisco. He's going to be a doctor. He is in-- on residence with Kaiser right now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we built a beautiful home, a beautiful family. And we are happy. And life has turned around to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have a sweet wife, God bless her. We get along. We, we understand each other. We love each other. And thank God, now we have six grandchildren. God bless them. And life is better. Life goes on. But that is the price I paid for being born Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fred, you've told us quite a story, an unbelievable story of, of your survival. What do you think it is about you that helped you survive this, this terrible, terrible tragedy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hope-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just I didn't let myself go. Because it was going bad one after the other, through worse to worse to worse. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was young, thank God. And that maybe helped a lot that I could push this-- all this troubles through somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what message would you like to leave for mankind, for your family, for generations to come, about what you have learned from, from your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I would like to say is we should keep up talking about those things. We should never forget it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just hope it never develops a things like that anymore. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Never again, never again, never again, things like that should happen. We got to keep on talking to our generation, to our children. They should know all about it. Show them pictures. Show them tapes. And they will learn from this experience what we went through in our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I like to say is that the Jewish people should just hope all the time. If they hope, they will succeed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they should not lose their mind. They should always be strong, support each other, give charity, believe in God. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If you believe in God, God will help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fred, I have to say that it's been an honor to be here with you today, to help you tell your story. And your survival has brought hope. You have a beautiful family. And this has been a, a very wonderful day for me to get to, to be with you. And I thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank you. And I hope that the generation will see and learn from the things I went through. And we should keep up talking about it and not-- never to forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Fred.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't have to slate?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. OK. Fred, who are you with here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am with my sweet, lovely wife, Miriam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Miriam, what would you like to say to Fred today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm, I'm really surprised that you handled the interview so well, even though I saw a few times tears. I'm ready to cry now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He did good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this man you married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he is-- he has a lot of optimism. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [LAUGHS] Thanks. Nothing took him down, really-- being all alone in the camps, being very sick, coming to a strange land with no language, just a trade, and his brother who was-- who told him he would help with in any way, his brother Herman, and the courage to come-- I mean, start all over again. He was two years in New York and then three years in Denver when I met him. And it's amazing to believe that out of ashes, he was reborn. And I say this to the grandkids. Because you know teenagers today, they get so disillusioned and confused. And they think everything is such a tragedy if they have a hangnail. And I al-- we always used Fred's story as an example that if he didn't have the hope to survive and that there was something bigger than we are, we wouldn't be here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're 12 people. Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fred, you can respond.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I am happy with my family. And I-- I'm proud of them. And I'm proud of them to see them to education and giving them a good education, which I couldn't have. And they all graduated, thank God. And they are doing just fine for themselves. And that is together. We worked together, me and my wife, on it very hard. And thank God, God helped us to accomplish all those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- he had the fortitude and the hope that when we our children-- Hanna, Sylvia, and Tony-- that if they will have a good education and a formal Jewish education in addition, so they know the foundation, the core of our faith, that they will always be able to stand up to any, any negative environment. And they did have some experiences in school. And up to today, even if it doesn't happen to them, it hap-- we read it in the paper about anti-Semitism. But they were always proud to be Jewish. They did not hide that. Not only they were proud to be Jewish, but they were proud to have parents who are from European--

speaker: interviewer
text: Background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Background. We were born in America. And they speak English. They learned to speak Greek through my parents. Because I came with my parents to the States. And they understand Yiddish. And they've learned other languages in school. And they read Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they are very well-rounded, contributing citizens in this country. They are not that the world owes them a living. They were able to give of themselves. They created a family, a new unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: All three of them, they married. Hanna married Mark Shaner. And together, they have three children. Sylvia married Alan Wolpa. And together, they have three children. Our son married [? Jeannie. ?] And they, they are just starting to think of a family. Because he just finished school. And he's going to be a podiatrist. And [? Jeannie is ?] working hard. And they're thinking of starting a family, too. So now we have six grandchildren, as of today. And we really are blessed in more than ways than we could express ourselves. The only other thing I would like to say is it's an unfortunate thing what happened with [INAUDIBLE] last night. And we are all pretty emotional, in addition to giving this interview. But if I was going to think of a today's hero that my children and our family ought to be grateful for, will be Steven Spielberg. Through he has done more making the world aware of the plight of the Holocaust survivors, that they want to be witnesses today, and the plight of every Jew in every country we're in, including Israel. Because Israel today is made primarily of Jews who came from every country in the world. Sure, there are some sabras. But as the world is evolving, we have Jews from all over the world, including the former Soviet Union, who go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So through, through through Steven Spielberg's foresight to put that on record, we are today the witnesses of something that is too big right now to, to digest. I cannot picture it to be in the internet and be able, 50 years from now, some young child in the Deep South or some child in Africa or some adult in Europe can push a button. And the Holocaust and the story and the witnesses of that will be in the picture. That is overwhelming. You feel so little when you can think of something so magnificent. And for you, Gary, for being our interviewer-- this is a mitzvah. And you know what a mitzvah is? I do. I thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big mitzvah, Gary. And I, I was very honored to be interviewed by you. And I hope--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, likewise, very likewise.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to show the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, we'll get to that in a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fred, let me ask you one final thing. And then we'll do the pictures. 38 years with Miriam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's it been like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- It is, it was, it is happiness all the time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I cannot express myself what a wonderful wife she is and how nice we built a wonderful family together. And I'm very proud of her. And I hope that we continue living long enough and see more and more grandchildren and great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we just lost my mom two years ago. So she was able to see great-grandchildren. And they lived in Denver. My both parents lived in Denver. So we were very close to them. And not only he was a son-in-law, he was a son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've built a beautiful family together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked hard on it. But we did it. And we are both happy. And, and, and I hope we will keep on going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you have a lot to be proud of.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you both. Fred, could you tell us about this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I say my name? This is me, Fred Hoffman, or Efraim Hoffman, on this picture. It was taken in Germany in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Pretty ?] [? good. ?] Stand by.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Fred. Please tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Efraim Hoffman. And it was taken in 1946 in Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Big ears.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Fred.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, again, Fred-- Efraim Fred Hoffman. And that was taken in 1949 in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: You should say you wish them a ha-- it was your first [INAUDIBLE] card.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you say there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Happy, happy [INAUDIBLE]. Happy Rosh Hashanah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Fred.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, Efraim, and my brother, Herman Hoffman. And that was picture taken in Germany in 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a great picture. Look how clear it is. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Although Herman's pompadour is a little-- Asher, [INAUDIBLE]. Asher.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Fred.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of [? Hanle ?] Asher, my sister Bessie, my brother, Herman, and Mia [? Frei, ?] taken in 1949 in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's son-in-- my daugh-- my brother-in-law. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All three are the same here. '56, August 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Fred?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of me, Efraim, and my brother, Herman, on our wedding day, in 19-- 8-- August 18, 1956.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Denver, Colorado.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to-- yeah, that doesn't sound very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we'll try that one again.

speaker: interviewee
text: The day of your wedding with your brother was the best man.

speaker: interviewee
text: So how shall I say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we on? On your wedding day-- go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken on my wedding day, August 18, 1956, my brother, Herman.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll start from the top again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take three.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, take three. Go, Fred.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken on my wedding day. My brother, my be-- this was my best man. I am Fred. He is Herman, in Denver, Colorado, August 18, 1956.

speaker: interviewer
text: These pictures may need to be frozen because of the black breaking up the signal.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Fred, could you tell us about this document please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this document was given to me after I passed the test of being a tailor from ORT in Germany in 1950. And with this document, I could come, enter, emigrate to America and have a profession. This is the picture which I met, met my wife through the newspaper when they came out to Denver. The name is Ce-- the grandmother is Cecilia [? Levy. ?] The, the mother on the left is Esther [? Nahmouli, ?] Miriam's brother, [? Zak ?] [? Nahmouli. ?] And then is Miriam. And then is [? Zak ?] [? Nahmouli, ?] the father, [? Itzhak. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was taken in 1956 in Denver, Colorado. You see, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can he talk?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Slavic religion, they can name the boy after the father while the father is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: In mine, I-- we could not.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, me neither.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Me neither. Huh. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So you see, we went back to Greece in 1990. I could tell whose son I was meeting without even knowing [INAUDIBLE], because they all had their father's name or their grandfather's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they don't put first or second, like we do in the Catholic or Protestant religion, like they say George II or junior, no. [? INT: ?] Interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son and his future wife, their engagement picture, taken in 1949-- in 1989, in Denver, Colorado. My son's name is Anthony Hoffman and [? Jeannie-- ?] [? Pillar. ?]
